entry.title: Hello SAP Community
entry.content:
Hi everyone! I’m Enzo from the Philippines.

I’m currently undergoing training on SAP PaPM Universal Model, and so far, it has been both challenging and exciting. SAP definitely has a steep learning curve, but that’s exactly what makes the journey rewarding—I’m constantly learning something new.

A bit about me: I graduated in October 2025 with a degree in Computer Science. My first professional experience was as a Quality Assurance Specialist Intern, where I focused mostly on software testing. I’m now working as a Quality Assurance Engineer (L1) in a different company. I honestly feel privileged (and a bit surprised!) to be working with SAP systems this early in my career. Although the concepts and processes can be overwhelming at times, I see it as a great opportunity to grow and expand my skills. I'm excited throughout my training as I'll gradually learn on how to use and apply SAP PaPM in real-world financial decision-making and scenarios. 

Looking forward to learning, sharing insights, and connecting with the SAP community. Nice to meet you all!

entry.title: From Sales Data to Design Blueprints: How RPT-1 Enabled Our Fashion Inverse Design Engine
entry.content:
What We Built

The Fashion Trend Alchemist is a fully functional inverse design engine that turns historical sales data into concrete design recommendations — complete with product visualizations, a catchy product name, and compelling sales copy. It works across product categories: coats, t-shirts, dresses, trousers, even shoes and sunglasses. The system adapts to any product type without retraining or reconfiguration.
 
How it creates a product — the full pipeline:
The product creation workflow: from context selection through AI enrichment and RPT-1 prediction to the final product profile with images, name, and sales text. The refine loop lets users iterate on any design decision.
As shown in the diagram above, product creation flows through four core phases:
1. Context Setup — Select a product category (e.g., men's winter coats), define filters and date ranges, and let the system build a curated context of top and bottom performers from the sales data
2. Enrichment — A Vision LLM analyzes product images to extract detailed design attributes (collar type, button style, fabric weight...) that don't exist in the raw database
3. Prediction — The user specifies which attributes to lock and which to predict; RPT-1 fills in the optimal values using in-context learning from the enriched dataset
4. Product Profile — The system generates multi-view product images, proposes a product name, and drafts sales copy — creating a complete, tangible design concept that can be refined and saved to a collection
 
Technical Architecture
This isn't a Jupyter notebook experiment — it's a deployed, interactive application where users explore design decisions in real time.
 
The Fashion Trend Alchemist runs on SAP BTP's Kyma runtime as a three-container deployment. The React frontend communicates with a Fastify/Node.js backend that orchestrates three AI services: RPT-1 for attribute prediction and GPT-4.1 for data enrichment and text generation (both via SAP AI Core), and a **self-hosted Z-Image Turbo** instance (product visualization, deployed as a Kyma microservice). PostgreSQL, Redis, and SeaweedFS handle data persistence, caching, and image storage respectively.
 
How It Works: Designing a Men's Winter Coat
Let's walk through a concrete example to see each phase in action: a product manager wants to know what the next successful men's winter coat should look like.
 
Building the Context
The user selects "Coats" as the product type, filters for winter seasons and men's wear, and the system queries a large transaction dataset for matching products.
For each matching product, the system calculates a velocity score — first computing units sold per day of availability (transaction count divided by days between first and last sale), then converting this to a percentile rank (0-100) across all matching products. A coat that sold 200 units in 30 days scores higher than one that sold 200 units over 6 months. The system then selects the top and bottom performers to create a balanced context showing RPT-1 clear examples of "success" and "failure."
 
Enriching Products with Design Attributes
Fashion databases store generic columns: color, material, product group. But a coat's success depends on collar type, insulation level, and closure style — attributes that don't exist in the raw data.

We solve this with AI enrichment. First, an LLM generates a product-specific attribute schema (e.g., `collar_type`, `button_style`, `insulation_level` with their possible values). Then, GPT-4.1 analyzes each product's image and extracts these attributes as structured data.
Real-time enrichment with progress tracking — GPT-4.1 extracts structured design attributes from product images.
 
RPT-1 in Action: The Prediction
Now the core moment. The enriched context — ~50 products with their detailed attributes and velocity scores — is sent to RPT-1. The user constructs a query row where some attributes are fixed and others contain `[PREDICT]` placeholders.
 
 
The interface uses a three-column layout:

- Locked (left): Attributes the user wants to fix — "I know I want a Navy coat"
- AI-Predicted (center): Attributes RPT-1 should determine — "What collar type? What button style?"
- Excluded (right): Attributes irrelevant to this prediction
 
The user sets a target success score and clicks "Transmute." RPT-1 receives the full context plus the query row in a single API call — no model training, no feature engineering, no pipeline orchestration. Formatted tabular data in, predictions out.

The entire ML layer is essentially: _format data as rows → add query row with `[PREDICT]` → call API → parse response_. The system complexity lives in data preparation, not model management.
 
Bringing It to Life: The Full Product Profile
The predicted attributes are combined with locked values to generate a complete product profile. The system translates the attribute combination into structured image prompts and produces multiple product views via a self-hosted image generation model running on Kyma — using category-aware prompt templates (coats use ghost mannequin photography; footwear uses product pair shots; accessories get contextual framing). This is completed by a catchy product name and a sales text.
 
The result isn't an abstract list of attributes — it's a concrete product concept that a designer or product manager can evaluate, iterate on, and refine. The refine loop shown in the pipeline diagram lets users adjust any attribute and regenerate until they're satisfied, then save the design to a collection.
 
What We Learned About RPT-1
Prediction Becomes a Data Formatting Problem
RPT-1 is pretrained and uses in-context learning — you don't train the model, you provide examples and a query. The API structure is remarkably simple:
The entire prediction integration is ~50 lines of TypeScript. No training scripts, no model serialization, no serving infrastructure. This drastically lowers the barrier for teams outside ML/data science — if you can format tabular data, you can use RPT-1.

Dynamic schemas come free: coats need `collar_type` and `button_style`, sunglasses need `lens_tint` and `frame_shape`. With traditional ML, changing features means re-encoding, retraining, revalidating. With RPT-1, just change the columns.
 
Validating RPT-1 in the Fashion Domain
Our goal wasn't to build a production prediction system — it was to test whether RPT-1's in-context learning could handle fashion data meaningfully. To validate this, we inverted the workflow: instead of predicting attributes from success scores (inverse design), we tested how well RPT-1 predicts success from attributes (forward prediction). This serves as a sanity check — if the model understands attribute-success relationships, it can leverage them for inverse predictions.

We benchmarked RPT-1 against traditional gradient boosting models (XGBoost, CatBoost) across five product categories using an 80/20 random split:
Model
R² 
NDCG 
Hit@25
RPT-1
+0.302
0.954
60.5%
XGBoost
+0.267
0.946
55.1%
CatBoost
+0.258
0.945
 56.4%
Aggregated across five product categories. RPT-1 matches established gradient boosting models on absolute fit (R²) and edges them out on ranking quality (NDCG, Hit@25) — the metrics that matter most for separating winners from losers.
 
The scatter plot below shows this concretely for ladies' dresses: top performers (Q4, green) cluster where predicted, while bottom performers (Q1, red) separate clearly. The model isn't perfect, but the quartile separation confirms RPT-1 successfully learned from the in-context examples.
Actual vs. predicted rank for Ladies All-Season Dresses (ρ = 0.626). Clear quartile separation demonstrates RPT-1 effectively distinguishes high from low performers using only in-context examples.
 
The key finding: RPT-1 worked out-of-the-box for fashion data without domain-specific tuning. For teams exploring tabular foundation models in new domains, this suggests the pretrained capabilities transfer broadly. RPT-1's in-context learning architecture particularly shines in low-data scenarios — some niche categories had only ~23 items in the context, where RPT-1 handled these gracefully.
 
 
 
 
Context Quality Matters More Than Context Structure
We systematically tested what affects RPT-1's prediction quality:

Row and column ordering — virtually no measurable impact. Shuffling the context rows or reordering columns didn't change results.

Column naming — slight but inconsistent effect. Renaming attributes to synonyms, German translations, or even numeric codes showed minor variations, but no clear pattern.
Column naming — slight but inconsistent effect. Renaming attributes to synonyms, German translations, or even numeric codes showed minor variations, but no clear pattern.
 
Out-of-distribution contamination — high impact. When wrong products polluted the context (e.g., shirts mixed into a hoodies dataset), prediction quality degraded significantly.
 
Spearman correlation drops as OOD contamination increases from 0% to 40%. Clean data matters far more than clever formatting.

The takeaway: focus more on data cleaning and proper normalization, rather than perfecting the data structure.
 
Challenges We Faced and How We Solved Them
Real-World Data is Messy

Problem: Product catalogs contain labeling errors — shirts categorized as hoodies, skirts labeled as pants. In-context learning amplifies these errors: garbage in, garbage out.

Solution: Our Vision LLM enrichment returns a `mismatchConfidence` score (0-100) alongside extracted attributes. Products scoring above 80 are flagged for human review. The user sees a prefiltered list and can correct or exclude problematic items before prediction. What started as an afterthought became essential.

GenAI Output is Unpredictable

Problem: LLM outputs can be wrong or malformed — invalid JSON, attributes outside valid ranges, unexpected structures.

Solution: Multi-layer guardrails:

- Prompting with positive examples and explicit output structure
- Runtime validation with Zod schemas to catch structural errors
- Attribute values validated against the ontology's allowed options
- Retry logic with exponential backoff (up to 3 attempts)
 
Users can manually adjust results or send them back to the LLM with feedback.
 
Consistency Across Generated Images
Problem: Three product views (front, back, model) must show the same design. Different prompts lead to inconsistent products.

Solution: The LLM generates a single shared `productDescription` used as the consistent core across all views, combined with view-specific prefixes and category-aware templates (wearables use ghost mannequin photography; footwear uses product pair shots).
 
Closing

Three takeaways for your next project:

1. RPT-1 turns prediction into a data selection & formatting problem. No training pipeline, no ML expertise required — just clean tabular data with a success metric.

2. Context quality beats context structure. Row ordering and naming conventions barely matter; wrong data in the context matters enormously.

3. Build guardrails around GenAI. Validate LLM outputs with schemas, constrain responses to allowed values, and keep humans in the loop for edge cases.
 
 
Developed at BTP Solution Advisory APAC by Danylo Polishchuk and Nils Dittrich.
 
For questions or collaboration opportunities, connect with us on LinkedIn: Danylo Polishchuk | Nils Dittrich.
 
Want to see the Fashion Trend Alchemist in action? Check out the demo video for a walkthrough of the complete workflow.

Want to try RPT-1 yourself? Explore the RPT-1 Playground to test tabular predictions with your own data.

entry.title: Re: Widget to show a feed containing more than 15 items
entry.content: Hi @Robotperson, first of all it seems to be a as-is size limitation for content widget https://help.sap.com/docs/build-work-zone-advanced-edition/sap-build-work-zone-advanced-edition/content-widget?q=widget, then I think maybe there are two directions to further process your question/requirement: 1) Please go to the feature request channel to raise request to have paging for content widget (even it is accepted in the roadmap it will take time to release) ; 2)you might take some walkaround from UX side, for example, show the top 15 most recent or most liked blog, and put the message to say 'xx more' and the link for user to navigate to the original blog platform

entry.title: 通过 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 中的公司代码限制增强成本中心安全性
entry.content:
简介

安全和访问控制是ERP系统的基本支柱。随着组织不断发展且数据越来越敏感，对精细权限控制的需求变得至关重要。SAP S/4HANA Cloud 继续发展其权限框架，以满足这些关键业务需求。例如: Authorization on company code level for app Manage Cost Centers F1443A

权限体系结构的一个重要增强是引入基于公司代码的公司代码限制。此新功能代表向前迈出的重要一步，有助于组织更严格地控制可以跨不同业务实体访问和管理成本中心信息的人员。

在此博客中，我们将探索此权限增强、其对系统管理员的影响以及确保此功能在 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 即将发布的版本 (2608) 中处于激活状态时实现平稳转换所需的步骤。

场景：了解业务需求

成本中心访问控制的挑战

成本中心是用于在不同部门和业务单位之间分配成本、跟踪费用以及管理财务报表的关键组织结构。在具有多个公司代码的大型组织（代表不同的法律实体或业务部门）中，管理谁可以访问哪些成本中心变得越来越复杂。

考虑以下真实场景：

一家全球制造公司经营多个子公司，每个子公司都有自己的公司代码：

- 公司代码 1000（欧洲分部）

- 公司代码 2000（亚洲分部）

- 公司代码 3000（美洲分部）

每个部门都有自己的成本中心和财务经理。之前，在没有详细的公司代码限制的情况下，有权进行成本中心管理的用户可能会无意中访问所有公司代码的成本中心数据 - 可能会暴露敏感财务信息并产生合规性风险。

解决方案：为成本中心部署公司代码限制

新的基于公司代码的成本中心受限访问功能使管理员能够：

1.限制成本中心可见性：限制用户仅查看与其授权公司代码相关联的成本中心

2.控制修改权限：确保用户只能修改其已分配公司代码中的成本中心主数据

3.加强合规性：满足数据分离和访问控制方面的法规要求

4.改进审计跟踪：创建明确、可审计的记录，提供谁访问哪些成本中心的信息

实施详细信息

  1. 权限要求

要配置和管理公司代码限制，管理员必须具有特定权限：

所需业务目录：

- `SAP_CORE_BC_IAM_RM` - 此业务目录必须分配给负责身份和访问管理 (IAM) 的管理员

这可确保只有合格的 IAM 专业人员才能修改限制配置并防止对安全设置进行未经授权的更改。

 

  1.  配置步骤

管理员必须采取主动步骤来配置公司代码限制，然后才能成为强制限制。方法如下：

关键配置工具：

- 应用：维护业务角色 (F1492)

- 操作：为管理成本中心主数据的所有业务角色预配置公司代码限制值

配置流程：

1.打开维护业务角色应用 (F1492)

2.选择需要公司代码限制的业务角色

3.导航到限制配置部分

4.为限制分配相应的公司代码值

  1. 保存并激活配置

最佳实践：请在系统的低使用期间执行此配置，并在应用于生产系统之前在非生产环境中全面测试。

  1. 受影响的技术对象

公司代码限制将影响 SAP 系统多个层的访问控制：

CDS 视图（核心数据服务）：

- `I_CostCenter` – 主要成本中心主数据视图

- `I_CostCenterVH` – 成本中心值帮助视图

- `I_CostCenterSTDVH` – 成本中心标准值帮助

- `I_CostCenterTP_2` – 成本中心交易过账视图

数据字典值帮助：

- `KOST` – 成本中心的传统值帮助

业务对象接口：

- `I_CostCenterTP_2` – 业务对象处理成本中心事务

  1. 系统行为的预期更改

公司代码限制生效后，将发生以下更改：

访问限制：

- 不具备适当公司代码权限的用户将无法再查看已分配公司代码之外的成本中心主数据

- 尝试访问未经授权的成本中心将导致数据不可用

- 涉及成本中心选择的系统操作将根据公司代码权限过滤选项

对用户体验的影响：

- 值帮助下拉列表（F4 帮助）将仅显示已授权的成本中心

- 报表和分析将仅显示与已分配公司代码相关的数据

- 成本中心搜索将返回过滤结果

管理影响：

- 管理员必须确保所有受影响的业务角色都已配置公司代码限制

- 配置限制失败可能导致用户对之前可用数据的访问权限丢失

 配置时间表和建议

 第1阶段：立即操作（当前）

- 检查成本中心管理的当前权限设置

- 识别处理成本中心主数据的所有业务角色

— 审计当前用户访问模式以建立基本数据

 

 第 2 阶段：预配置（升级前）

- 使用维护业务角色应用 (F1492) 配置公司代码限制

- 为每个业务角色分配相应的公司代码值

- 开发/沙盒环境中的测试配置

 

 第 3 阶段：验证和测试

- 与每个公司代码的代表用户执行端到端测试

- 验证用户是否只能访问授权的成本中心

- 测试值帮助、报表和事务访问

— 记录任何权限访问的问题或意外行为

 

 第 4 阶段：部署（发布时）

- 将更新部署到生产系统

- 监控系统性能和用户反馈

- 及时解决任何访问问题

- 维护变更文档

 

有关此增强的附加信息：

- SAP 注释3632368 - Restricted Access to Cost Centers Based on Company Code - SAP for Me

- SAP 文档: Restricted Access to Cost Centers Based on Company Code | SAP Help Portal

 结论

成本中心主数据管理中引入公司代码限制是 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 权限功能的很有意义进步。使组织能够基于公司代码分配实施精细访问控制，此功能可以增强安全性、改进合规性并降低意外数据暴露的风险。

 

关键要点：

1.主动操作至关重要：不要等到配置公司代码限制时必须使用此功能。现在使用维护业务角色应用预配置值。

2.需要全面计划：审计当前权限结构，识别受影响的业务角色，并提前计划配置策略。

3.测试至关重要：在部署到生产之前彻底测试非生产环境中的配置，以防止用户访问问题。

4.管理监督：确保具有相应权限 (SAP_CORE_BC_IAM_RM) 的管理员管理这些配置以维护安全性。

5.持续合规：随着组织结构和用户角色的发展，定期审核和维护公司代码限制。

对于致力于保持强大的安全控制和法规合规性的组织，此增强完全符合身份和访问管理的最佳实践。通过采用结构化、计划良好的实施方法，管理员可以在即将到来的 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 版本中强制实施公司代码限制时确保无缝过渡。

 

现在是行动的时候了。立即开始预配置工作，以确保此关键权限增强生效时的业务连续性和安全性。

后续步骤：

- 计划检查当前成本中心权限结构

- 识别需要公司代码限制配置的业务角色

- 规划实施时间表，与即将进行的系统更新保持一致

- 联系 SAP 系统管理员，开始预配置工作

entry.title: Manage Purchase Contracts App – Output Details Greyed Out in Output Control (S/4HANA Public Cloud)
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

 We are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and trying to download the XML data for a Purchase Contract using the Manage Purchase Contracts app.

Following SAP documentation/notes, we attempted to change the Output Details in Output Control → Items so that we can enter the code:

 
#&&DOWNLOAD_FORM_DATA_XML&&#

in the Print Queue to download the XML form data.

However, the Output Details fields are greyed out and cannot be edited.

What we have checked so far:

  • Created new purchase contract documents to ensure it is not old data

  • Verified the Output Status is "In Preparation"

  • Checked that the output item exists in Output Control

  • Despite this, Output Details remains non-editable

Because of this, we cannot insert the required code #&&DOWNLOAD_FORM_DATA_XML&&# into the print queue to download the XML.

Question

Has anyone encountered this issue before?
Is there any configuration, authorization, or output parameter setting that could cause the Output Details to be greyed out in the Manage Purchase Contracts app?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Best practice: SAP Ariba Analytical Reporting API
entry.content:
API modes at a glance

  • Synchronous
    • Paginated: up to 50 records per page.
    • Max 300 pages/day → 50 × 300 = 15,000 records/day.
    • Provides a /count endpoint that returns the total number of results for a view template + filters.
    • Best for very small, ad-hoc queries (few fields, narrow date ranges).
  • Asynchronous
    • Output delivered in ZIP files: 5,000 records per file.
    • Up to 10 files per job → 50,000 records/job.
    • Files expire 48 hours after creation.
    • Up to 40 async jobs/day → theoretical max ≈ 2,000,000 records/day.
    • Jobs are also paginated, meaning if the count returns 80,000 records, 2nd jobs with same filters can be submitted for the 30,000 records. (1st job capped at 50,000 records).
    • Best for bulk exports and scheduled ETL.

Rate limits and important behavior

  • Each API response includes rate-limit headers showing remaining rate-limit per second, minute, hour, and day. Measure and log these values so clients can adapt dynamically. You will never hit 429 error by comparing the last time call was made, what is remain, and when you can start calling again.
  • If a short-window limit (e.g., per-minute) is exceeded you’ll receive HTTP 429. A 429 response still counts as one call toward larger windows (hour/day). Repeated 429s therefore consume broader quotas quickly.
  • Design retry logic with exponential backoff and respect rate-limit headers; avoid tight retry loops after 429s.

Use /count intelligently

  • Call the /count end point (with the same view template and filters) to learn result size before paging.
  • If the /count end point indicates many records, switch to asynchronous export or narrow the filters. This prevents your synchronous page/day quota from being exhausted unnecessarily.

Why synchronous can be problematic in shared/on-demand scenarios

  • Synchronous paging is limited and expensive in aggregate. If multiple users repeatedly run on-demand synchronous extracts for similar datasets, they will quickly deplete shared rate limits (per-second/minute/hour/day), causing 429s and impacting other consumers.
  • Use a central ETL or async exports for shared or repeated large queries.

Concise best practices

  • Pick the right mode: synchronous for tiny interactive checks; asynchronous for bulk loads or scheduled ETL.
  • Use the /count end point first to decide the mode.
  • Minimize payloads: only select required fields and use tight filters or incremental patterns.
  • Monitor and log rate-limit headers (remaining per second/minute/hour/day).
  • Retry policy: exponential backoff with capped attempts; after 429, back off and re-check rate limit remaining in headers.
  • Automate immediate downloads; async files expire after 48 hours.
  • Orchestrate jobs to stagger jobs and respect the 40-job/day cap.

Example workflows 

  • Interactive UI check (synchronous): call the /count end point → if count small, fetch pages and display; else prompt background async export.
  • Nightly datawarehouse sync (asynchronous): schedule job for daily deltas → poll until complete → download ZIPs → ingest within 48 hours.

Use the /count end point to avoid costly synchronous paging, prefer asynchronous jobs for bulk ETL, and always measure/adapt to the per-second/minute/hour/day limits returned in responses. Avoid letting many users run on-demand synchronous extracts against the same data; centralize extraction or use async exports to preserve rate limit.

Extras: For Operational reporting for Procurement or Sourcing, the same practice should be followed. The only difference is the number of jobs and the maximum records per file for Asynchronous call/mode.

Operational Reporting — Procurement vs Sourcing

  • Asynchronous outputs use smaller files: 1,000 records per file.
  • Each job can produce up to 10 files → max 10,000 records/job.
  • Daily job limits differ:
    • Procurement: 40 async jobs/day → theoretical max 400,000 records/day (40 × 10,000).
    • Sourcing: 150 async jobs/day → theoretical max 1,500,000 records/day (150 × 10,000).

entry.title: Why Compound Employee Delta Integrations May Behave Differently After ONB 2.0 Migration
entry.content:
Introduction

The move from ONB 1.0 to ONB 2.0 results in a major architectural shift in the underlying data model of Onboarding. This change has downstream impacts even on existing integrations consuming Employee Central data. Many organisations assume that since Employee Central itself is not being reconfigured, any integrations to and from it are safe. However, as we will see, this assumption can sometimes lead to unexpected failures.

This blog describes how an organisation’s long-standing payroll integration pulling employee master data from EC failed after migration to ONB 2.0, even though it had no direct dependency on the Onboarding module.

Integration Failure After ONB 2.0 Migration

A customer’s go-live after migration to ONB 2.0 appeared smooth. However, one of their Payroll integrations failed in the first few runs post-migration. The integration was designed to retrieve new hire data from Employee Central via the Compound Employee API, process it and send to their third-party Payroll system.

The error message at the XSLT mapping step indicated that the mapping expected a single National ID Card record to be present in the payload. This error resulted from there being two records instead.

If this were a possibility, then why did the integration never fail before? Why now, after so many years?

The reason lies in an important architectural change introduced with the move to ONB 2.0 from ONB 1.0. In ONB 2.0, onboarding now uses the same underlying data model as Employee Central. This also gives rise to a well-known issue where queries to EC entities now begin returning data for onboarding candidates in addition to hired employees. The SAP KBA 3017752 explains that this can be resolved by including the hiringNotCompleted field in the query filter set to false so that the Compound Employee API returns only data of hired employees.

Our issue, however, had a different cause.

Why the Payload Behaviour Changed

The delta integration in question processed new hires (those hired since the previous day’s integration run) and sent their details to the Payroll system. Since the new hire data was processed within a day of the hire, it was reasonable to assume that the data would not be modified in any way within this short span.

In payload terms, this meant that the Compound Employee response would contain only one record per portlet, one with Action=INSERT. This was apparently a reasonable assumption since it had been working reliably for years.

This behaviour changed with the move to ONB 2.0 as data for new hires was now created way earlier in EC under ONB 2.0 as compared to ONB 1.0. In ONB 2.0, onboarding candidates are assigned a user ID and their data is mapped to and populated in the core EC entities (PerPersonal, EmpEmployment, JobInformation etc.) as soon as onboarding is initiated. Because this data is written to EC when onboarding begins rather than when the candidate is finally hired (after completion of Manage Pending Recruits step), there is now a longer time window during which the candidate’s data may be modified. Addresses may be corrected, HR may format the contact information, Job Information may be modified, and National ID may be deleted and a different one created. 

Since the data lifecycle from EC’s perspective now begins way before the actual hiring (at Onboarding initiation to be exact), all this history of changes will be reflected in the Compound Employee payload. This means that the delta integration may now receive multiple records per portlet with a mix of INSERT, CHANGE and DELETE action types depending on how the data was modified since Onboarding triggered. Previously, the integration did not have to iterate through multiple records per portlet and neither had to deal with nested “Previous” nodes which may now be present in the payload.

Conclusion

Ultimately, it all depends on how the payload from Compound Employee API is processed. If the integration has already been designed to accommodate the presence of multiple records with different Action types, then all is well.

However, the point to keep in mind is that migration to ONB 2.0 requires thorough testing prior to going live. Just because it is ‘only’ the Onboarding module that is changing and EC has not been reconfigured, it is tempting to assume that EC related integrations will not be impacted. Practically, the data lifecycle within EC has fundamentally shifted.

Integrations must be reviewed and, if required, adapted to handle multiple action types per portlet, nested ‘previous’ nodes and the presence of hiringNotCompleted users in the incoming payloads. Testing with realistic ONB 2.0 scenarios is essential, particularly for payroll related flows where integration downtimes are high-risk.

entry.title: Re: How to calculate bonus proration in Variable Pay usin...
entry.content:
@azhicon  based on your latest reply to @DavidSomelofske yes you can by adding a column to the summary level of VarPay with a lookup formula in it but it's difficult because it requires customization down the line to make it work for people whose total number of days isn't equal to 365 or 366.

For those you will need to calculate the number of days for each record retrieved from EC using ALCF (assignment level custom fields). Then aggregate it up at summary level and substract the number you loaded through lookup table so you get an accurate number of eligible days. Using a rule of 3 with the number of days and the payout that was already calculated before applying the LOA day from your lookup table you can get to the correct payout. I've built this before for a customer in retail.

Complicated but within reach of any certified consultant with 2/3 projects under their belt.

I've documented ALCF extensively in some of my blog like here (#3) or even in this blog here.

In this case you need to import standard start-date and end-date into ALCF field of type date then do a datediff formula (see screenshot).

Best,

Xavier

entry.title: Bridging the Gap: Bringing OPF to SAP Commerce Accelerator Storefronts
entry.content:
Introduction

Many SAP Commerce customers still have JSP-based Accelerator storefronts, which often include custom payment integrations. As the Open Payment Framework (OPF) has focused on Spartacus-driven storefronts, a significant gap has emerged for organizations not yet prepared to migrate. This has resulted in challenges for both customers and partners, including delayed innovation, increased maintenance costs, and missed opportunities for modernizing checkout experiences.

SAP Commerce Cloud, open payment framework SDK

To bridge this gap, SAP has developed an Open Source tool - SAP Commerce Cloud, open payment framework SDK- that extends OPF capabilities to JSP-based Accelerator storefronts. This solution allows customers to leverage OPF’s low/no-code payment configuration without requiring an immediate front-end migration. The add-on decouples payment orchestration from the presentation layer, enabling standardized, agile payment integrations and reducing reliance on custom development.

This addon addresses current market realities where many customers are postponing Spartacus migrations due to various constraints, while the payment landscape continues to evolve with new providers, methods, and compliance requirements. Partners benefit from a unified, scalable approach to payments, which minimizes project risk and accelerates value delivery regardless of storefront technology.

SAP Commerce Cloud, open payment framework SDK simplifies payment integrations, streamlines onboarding of payment service providers, and reduces maintenance overhead. The approach supports rapid adoption of new payment methods, smoother transitions for legacy Accelerator customers, and flexible provider management without disruptive system changes. For partners, it offers faster implementation, reduced technical debt, and the opportunity to expand their service offerings through structured modernization packages and ongoing managed services.

Implementation involves assessing the current payment environment, installing and configuring the Accelerator Addon, onboarding providers through OPF, and iterative optimization and rollout. Common use cases supported by the addon include enabling “Buy Now, Pay Later” options, supporting multi-provider strategies, facilitating regional expansion, and optimizing provider performance.

Summary

The OPF Accelerator Addon empowers SAP Commerce customers to modernize their payment capabilities immediately while preserving future migration flexibility. By centralizing and standardizing payment integration, organizations can enhance agility, reduce costs, and simplify compliance, all without the need for an immediate front-end overhaul. Partners are positioned to deliver greater value through faster, lower-risk projects and differentiated service offerings, paving the way for smoother future transitions to Spartacus or other modern storefront technologies.

entry.title: Fiori Tile Translation using SE63 and SLXT
entry.content:
Introduction: This comprehensive guide walks you through the complete process of translating SAP Fiori tiles and ensuring those translations are properly managed through your SAP transport landscape. Whether you're supporting a multinational organization or expanding into new markets, this step-by-step process will help you deliver localized user experiences that drive business value. When users see interface elements in their native language, they're more comfortable, productive, and less likely to make errors.

  • SE63 – to perform the translation

  • SLXT – to capture the translation changes in a TR

1. Open T-Code SE63.
You will see the main translation screen. From the top toolbar, choose:
Short Texts → Meta Objects → TABL: Tables (Meta)

2. In the Object Name, enter:
WDY_CONF_USERT2
Then select the Source Language and Target Language, and click Edit.

3. In the next screen, use F4 help on the Configuration ID field.

4. In the description field, enter your Catalog ID with wildcards:
*<catalog_id>*
Then press Enter.

5. You will see a list of configuration IDs.
Open them one by one until you find the tile you want to translate.

6. Once you identify the correct configuration ID, click Execute.

7. If the tile name appears as expected, you can now enter the Target Language Translation.
After completing the translation, Save your changes.

Capture Translation in Transport Using SLXT

1. Open T-Code SLXT.

Target Language – choose the language you translated into

  1. In the Export Module section:

    • Individual Language – choose this option

    • Create Language Transport – choose this option

  2. In the Transport Request section:

    • Create a new one

    • Provide a meaningful Description

    • Request Type should be Workbench Request

  3. In the Processing Time section:

    • Set Processing Date (usually the current date)

  4. Under Filter, set:

    • Object Processor – enter your user ID

  5. Finally, click Execute.

Once saved, you will see a confirmation screen showing the successful translation.

Your transport will now be created.
You can check it in SE10 using the description you provided.

your Fiori tile translation will be available in the target language.

Conclusion: 

You've successfully mastered the art of Fiori tile translation and transport management. This process you've just completed is more than just changing text you've created a foundation for delivering truly global SAP experiences.

Best Practices to Remember:

  • Always test translations in a development environment first
  • Document your catalog IDs and configuration mappings for future reference
  • Consider creating a translation glossary to maintain consistency

Pro Tip: If translations don't appear immediately, remember to run the report /UI2/PAGE_CACHE_SYNCHRONIZE to refresh the translation cache and ensure your changes are visible to users.

entry.title: Collaborative Draft in SAP RAP Managed Scenario Sales Order Application
entry.content:
This blog explains hoe Collaborative Draft works in RAP Managed Scenario with a Sales Order Application.

A Collaborative Draft allows multiple users to work on  the draft instance of  a business object before it is finally activated. This is very useful in scenarios like Sales Order Processing etc. where different user such as sales excutives, managers, or finance team may update different parts pf document.

A Draft is a temporary version of business data that allows users to edit records without immediately saving them to the active database table.

Draft ensures:

  • Data is saved temporarily

  • Other users cannot overwrite active data

  • Changes can be reviewed before activation

In RAP, draft functionality is enabled using:

with draft

Collaborative Draft allows multiple users to work on the same draft instance simultaneously.

Instead of locking the document for a single user, RAP enables controlled collaboration where users can:

with collaborative draft

  • Edit different sections of the same document

  • Save draft changes

  • Activate the final version

This improves productivity and avoids duplicate documents.

RAP Architecture for Sales Order

The RAP model contains:

  1. Database Tables -> 

1)zdb_so_hdr

@EndUserText.label : 'Sales Order Header Table'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zdb_so_hdr {

  key client      : abap.clnt not null;
  key soid        : sysuuid_x16 not null;
  sorder          : abap.numc(10);
  partner         : abap.numc(10);
  status          : abap.char(1);
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_so_hdr.cuky'
  price           : abap.curr(10,2);
  cuky            : abap.cuky;
  created_by      : syuname;
  created_at      : timestampl;
  last_changed_by : syuname;
  last_changed_at : timestampl;
}
2)zdb_product

@EndUserText.label : 'Products'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zdb_product {

  key client : abap.clnt not null;
  key prod   : abap.char(20) not null;
  descr      : abap.char(40);
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_product.cuky'
  price      : abap.curr(10,2);
  cuky       : abap.cuky;

}

3)Draft Table->zdb_so_hdr01

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZI_SO_HDR'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zdb_so_hdr01 {

  key client    : abap.clnt not null;
  key soid      : sysuuid_x16 not null;
  sorder        : abap.numc(10);
  partner       : abap.numc(10);
  status        : abap.char(1);
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_so_hdr01.cuky'
  price         : abap.curr(10,2);
  cuky          : abap.cuky;
  createdby     : syuname;
  createdat     : timestampl;
  lastchangedby : syuname;
  lastchangedat : timestampl;
  "%admin"      : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;

}
4)zdb_status

@EndUserText.label : 'Status'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zdb_status {

  key client : abap.clnt not null;
  key status : abap.char(1) not null;
  text       : abap.char(40);

}
5)zdb_partner

@EndUserText.label : 'Partner'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zdb_partner {

  key client  : abap.clnt not null;
  key partner : abap.numc(10) not null;
  name        : abap.char(40);
  type        : abap.char(1);

}

5)zdb_currency

@EndUserText.label : 'CURRENCY'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zdb_currency {

  key client : abap.clnt not null;
  key cuky   : abap.cuky not null;

}

2. CDS Views (Interface + Projection)

1)Sales Order Interface View->ZI_SO_HDR

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Order Interface View'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZI_SO_HDR as select from zdb_so_hdr
association [0..1] to ZDD_PARTNER as _Partner on $projection.Partner = _Partner.Partner
association [0..1] to ZDD_Status as _Status on $projection.Status = _Status.Status
association [0..1] to ZDD_CURRENCY as _Currency on $projection.Cuky = _Currency.PriceUnit
{
    key soid as Soid,
    sorder as Sorder,
    partner as Partner,
    status as Status,
    @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'cuky'
    price as Price,
    cuky as Cuky,
    @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
    created_by as CreatedBy,
    @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
    created_at as CreatedAt,
    @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
    last_changed_by as LastChangedBy,    
    @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
    last_changed_at as LastChangedAt,
    _Partner,
    _Status,
    _Currency
}

2)Consumtion View -> ZC_SO_HDR

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Consumtion View'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Search.searchable: true
@UI: {
headerInfo: { typeName: 'Order', typeNamePlural: 'Orders',
title: { type: #STANDARD, value: 'SOrderNo', label: 'Order:' } } }
define root view entity ZC_SO_HDR
  as projection on ZI_SO_HDR
{

      @ui.facet: [ { id:              'Order',
                     purpose:         #STANDARD,
                     type:            #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
                     label:           'Order',
                     position:        10 } ]

      @ui.hidden: true

  key Soid,

      @ui: {
      lineItem:       [ { position: 10, importance: #HIGH } ],
      identification: [ { position: 10, label: 'Order No.' } ] }
      @search.defaultSearchElement: true

      Sorder       as SOrderNo,

      @ui: {
      lineItem:       [ { position: 20, importance: #HIGH } ],
      identification: [ { position: 20, label: 'Customer' } ],
      selectionField: [ { position: 20 } ] }
      @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity : {name: 'ZDD_PARTNER', element: 'Partner'  } }]
      @ObjectModel.text.element: ['Name']
      @search.defaultSearchElement: true
      Partner      as Customer,
      _Partner.Name,

      @ui: {
        lineItem:       [ { position: 30, importance: #HIGH } ],
        identification: [ { position: 30, label: 'Status' } ] }
      @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity : {name: 'ZDD_STATUS', element: 'Status'  } }]
      @ObjectModel.text.element: ['Text']
      Status       as Status,
      _Status.Text as Text,
      @ui: {
          lineItem:       [ { position: 40, importance: #HIGH } ],
          identification: [ { position: 40, label: 'Total Price' } ] }
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency'
      Price,
      @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{entity: {name: 'ZDD_CURRENCY', element: 'PriceUnit' }}]
      Cuky         as Currency,
      @ui: {
      lineItem:       [ { position: 50, importance: #MEDIUM } ],
      identification: [ { position: 50, label: 'Created By' } ] }
      CreatedBy,
      @ui.hidden: true
      CreatedAt,
      @ui.hidden: true
      LastChangedBy,
      @ui.hidden: true
      LastChangedAt,
      /* Associations */
      _Currency,
      _Partner,
      _Status
}

3) ZDD_Status

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Status'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Search.searchable: true
define view entity ZDD_Status
  as select from zdb_status
{
      @search.defaultSearchElement: true
      @ObjectModel.text.element: [ 'Text' ]
  key status as Status,
      @search.defaultSearchElement: true
      @search.fuzzinessThreshold: 0.8
      @Semantics.text: true
      text   as Text
}
4)ZDD_PARTNER

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'partner View'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Search.searchable: true
define view entity ZDD_PARTNER as select from zdb_partner
{

@Search.defaultSearchElement: true
    key partner as Partner,
    
    @search.defaultSearchElement: true
    @search.fuzzinessThreshold: 0.8
    @Semantics.text: true
    name as Name,
    type as Type
}
5)ZDD_CURRENCY

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Currency View'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Search.searchable: true
define view entity ZDD_CURRENCY
  as select from zdb_currency
{
      @search.defaultSearchElement: true
  key cuky as PriceUnit
}

6)ZDD_QUERRY_01

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Query draft'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZDD_QUERRY_01
  as select from zdb_so_hdr01
{
  key soid                          as Soid,
      sorder                        as Sorder,
      partner                       as Partner,
      status                        as Status,
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Cuky'
      price                         as Price,
      cuky                          as Cuky,
      createdby,
      createdat,
      lastchangedby,
      lastchangedat,
      draftentitycreationdatetime   as Draftentitycreationdatetime,
      draftentitylastchangedatetime as Draftentitylastchangedatetime,
      draftadministrativedatauuid   as Draftadministrativedatauuid,
      draftentityoperationcode      as Draftentityoperationcode,
      hasactiveentity               as Hasactiveentity,
      draftfieldchanges             as Draftfieldchanges
}

3. Behavior Definition

managed implementation in class zbp_i_so_hdr unique;
strict ( 2 );
with collaborative draft;
extensible;
define behavior for ZI_SO_HDR alias SalesHeader
persistent table zdb_so_hdr
extensible
draft table zdb_so_hdr01 query ZDD_QUERRY_01
etag master LastChangedAt
lock master total etag LastChangedAt
authorization master ( instance )
{
  field ( readonly, numbering : managed ) Soid;
  field ( readonly ) Sorder, CreatedBy, CreatedAt, LastChangedBy, LastChangedAt;
  field ( mandatory ) Partner, Status, Cuky;
  create;
  update;
  delete;

  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Discard;
  draft action Edit;
  draft action Resume;
  draft determine action Prepare;
  draft action Share;

  determination GenerateOrderNumber on save { create; }

  }
projection implementation in class zbp_i_so_hdr unique;
strict ( 2 );
extensible;
use collaborative draft;
use side effects;
define behavior for ZC_SO_HDR alias SalesHeader
extensible
{
  use create;
  use update;
  use delete;

  use action Edit;
  use action Activate;
  use action Discard;
  use action Resume;
  use action Prepare;
  use action Share;
}

4. Behavior Implementation class

CLASS zcl_psh_helper DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES: if_oo_adt_classrun.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.



CLASS zcl_psh_helper IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.

    DATA: lt_currency TYPE TABLE OF zdb_currency.

    lt_currency = VALUE #(
      ( cuky = 'EUR' )
      ( cuky = 'USD' )
      ( cuky = 'INR' )
    ).

    DATA: lt_partner TYPE TABLE OF zdb_partner.

    lt_partner = VALUE #(
      ( partner = '1000011111' name = 'New World IT' type = 'C' )
      ( partner = '1000011112' name = 'North IT'     type = 'C' )
      ( partner = '1000011113' name = 'Global IT'    type = 'K' )
      ( partner = '1000011114' name = 'Central IT'   type = 'K' )
    ).

    DATA: lt_product TYPE TABLE OF zdb_product.

    lt_product = VALUE #(
      ( prod = 'Cloud_Package' descr = 'Cloud Package' price = '100' cuky = 'INR' )
      ( prod = 'Cloud_Office'  descr = 'Cloud Office'  price = '99'  cuky = 'EUR' )
      ( prod = 'Cloud_Trail'   descr = 'Cloud Trial'   price = '100' cuky = 'USD' )
    ).

    DATA: lt_status TYPE TABLE OF zdb_status.

    lt_status = VALUE #(
      ( status = 'O' text = 'Open' )
      ( status = 'I' text = 'InProcess' )
      ( status = 'C' text = 'Completed' )
      ( status = 'R' text = 'Rejected' )
      ( status = 'B' text = 'Blocked' )
    ).

    "Clear existing data
    DELETE FROM zdb_currency.
    DELETE FROM zdb_partner.
    DELETE FROM zdb_product.
    DELETE FROM zdb_status.

    "Insert fresh test data
    INSERT zdb_currency FROM TABLE @LT_currency.
    INSERT zdb_partner  FROM TABLE @LT_partner.
    INSERT zdb_product  FROM TABLE @LT_product.
    INSERT zdb_status   FROM TABLE @LT_status.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.
CLASS lhc_SalesHeader DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION
      IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR SalesHeader RESULT result.

    METHODS GenerateOrderNumber FOR DETERMINE ON SAVE
      IMPORTING keys FOR SalesHeader~GenerateOrderNumber.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_SalesHeader IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD get_instance_authorizations.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD GenerateOrderNumber.

    READ ENTITIES OF zi_so_hdr IN LOCAL MODE ENTITY SalesHeader
    FIELDS ( Sorder ) WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_sorder).

    DELETE lt_sorder WHERE sorder IS NOT INITIAL.
    IF lt_sorder IS NOT INITIAL.

      SELECT SINGLE FROM zdb_so_hdr FIELDS MAX( sorder ) INTO @DATA(lv_sorder_max).

      MODIFY ENTITIES OF zi_so_hdr IN LOCAL MODE ENTITY SalesHeader
      UPDATE FIELDS ( Sorder )
      WITH VALUE #( FOR Ls_sorder IN lt_sorder INDEX INTO i ( %key = ls_sorder-%key
      Sorder = lv_sorder_max + i ) )

      REPORTED DATA(lt_reported).

    ENDIF.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

5. Service Definition

@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Order'
define service ZSD_SalesORDER {
  expose ZC_SO_HDR as SalesOrder;
}

6. Service Binding

Conclusion

Collaborative Draft in SAP RAP Managed Scenario enables multiple users to work efficiently on the same business document without data conflicts.
Using Sales Order as an example, we saw how RAP allows draft creation, collaboration, and activation using CDS views and behavior definitions.

entry.title: How to add Information popup with above 73 characters length
entry.content:
How to add Information popup with above 73 characters length and how to add custom button in Table maintenance generator. 

Requirement 

Let’s take a scenario I created a custom button in TMG screen. After clicking on custom button I need to show tabstrip with table control. Inside the table control, I added a push button. If I click on More Info push button it will show message pop_up with above 73 character length. 


I Created a TMG. 

Whenever we want to add any custom button we need to add one event. 

Give form routine name. 

After changing the form routine. We need to change the function group as well. Go to SM30.  

Here I already changed the Program(GUI). That is the reason it is showing SAPLZMR_FNGP. 

Previously it was like SAPLSVIM. 

Now let’s see how Program(GUI) is changed. Go to SE41. Click on User Interface. Give From  program as SAPLSVIM and in to program give the form routine name which you added in events. 

Here add the new icon that is ‘CC’. Double click on the CC. 



Now the newly button is added.

Click on the Consistency check button. It will the details like this. 

If the customer wants to know the details of header and items details. I introduced new button called MORE_INFO. If the customer click on more info it will display the information with pop_up. 

Let’s Go to the coding. 

Go to the table, click on TMG and click on the overview screen number. 

Here I added the new Module( MODULE consistency_check.) 


Go to SE80. 

 


 




Click on screen 004. add select query to fetch the header data for displaying the data in table control.

Click on screen 005. add select query to fetch the item data for displaying the data in table control.



Conclusion: By using this pop_up if we want to create pop_up with above 1000 characters it is possible. we can display the message to the customers. so by using this pop_up they will get the idea about the requirement.

entry.title: My SAP Business AI Certification Journey
entry.content:
When I started these SAP Business AI courses (Discovering SAP Business AI, Positioning SAP Business AI and Boosting AI-driven Business Transformation with Joule Agents), I expected a standard product overview. What I got instead was a completely new way of thinking about enterprise technology, AI strategy, and how to have meaningful conversations with clients about digital transformation.

This blog is my attempt to share what I learned, how I passed my certification, and what surprised me along the way — for anyone about to take the same journey.

SAP Certification Name: SAP Certified - Positioning SAP Business AI Solutions as part of SAP Business Suite (C_BCBAI)

1. What the Certification Covers

The certification spans three SAP Learning courses, each building on the previous one. Together they cover the full SAP Business AI story — from strategy to technical architecture to real-world deployment.

Course

Focus

Key Learning

Discovering SAP Business AI

Why AI? What is SAP's strategy?

The SAP Flywheel — how Applications, Data and AI reinforce each other

Positioning SAP Business AI

How to position AI to different personas

Joule, AI Foundation, Responsible AI, Sustainability — and how each lands with CIO, CFO, COO

Joule Agents & Transformation

How AI agents actually work in business

Agentic AI, Business Data Cloud, Knowledge Graph, AI Agent Hub — the full technical picture

2. The Three Things That Changed How I Think

A. AI Without Data Is Just Noise

The biggest insight from Course 1 was simple but profound: AI is only as good as the data feeding it. Before these courses, I used to talk about AI features. Now I start every conversation with the data foundation.

SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) is what makes AI reliable — connecting all systems using zero-copy federation, preserving business context through the Semantic Layer, and keeping data sovereign in each region. Without BDC, AI outputs are guesswork.

B. Joule Is Not Just a Chatbot

Before the certification, I described Joule as "SAP's AI assistant." After it, I describe it very differently. Joule is an omnipresent AI Copilot with 1,800+ skills, covering 80% of SAP's most-used transactions, available in 11 languages, and capable of proactively acting before users even ask.

And then there are Joule Agents — a completely different level. Agents reason, plan, and execute multi-step workflows autonomously. The Cash Management Agent saves finance teams up to 70% of manual reconciliation time. The Production Planning Agent autonomously checks material and capacity. 

C. Responsible AI Is a Commercial Accelerator

I used to treat ethics and compliance as a defensive topic — something to address when clients raised concerns. The certification changed that. SAP's Responsible AI framework is actually a competitive advantage.

  • Client data never leaves SAP's governed architecture — never used to train third-party models
  • Every Joule action is fully auditable — no black-box decisions
  • Data sovereignty built in — GDPR in Europe, PDPA in Asia, automatic
  • ISO 42001 certified for AI governance — internationally recognised standard
  • Human-in-the-loop by design — AI recommends, humans approve critical decisions.

3. How I Applied the Learning in the Certification

The certification assessment is scenario-based — which I found genuinely challenging and genuinely useful. Here is what helped me prepare and what I would tell anyone taking it next.

Scenario Type

What It Tests

My Approach

Client objection handling

Can you position BDC and Joule against a "we don't need to change" mindset?

Memorised the control tower analogy — ERPs are engines, BDC is the control tower. Never replace, always layer.

Persona-based positioning

Which message lands with which persona — CIO vs CFO vs COO?

Built a mental matrix: CIO = integration + security, CFO = ROI + reconciliation time, COO = supply chain speed

Technical architecture

How does zero-copy federation work? What is the Knowledge Graph?

Used real examples: Knowledge Graph maps PO to Supplier to Invoice — grounding AI in business context, preventing hallucinations

Responsible AI scenarios

What happens to client data? How is compliance maintained?

Led with ISO 42001, data sovereignty, human-in-the-loop — not features, but governance principles

The exam rewards consultative thinking, not product recall. The question is rarely "what does Joule do?" It is almost always "given this client situation, what would you recommend and why?" Thinking like a consultant — not a salesperson — is the key.

4. What I Gained Beyond the Certificate

Certifications give you a credential. Good certifications give you a new lens. This one gave me both.

  • Confidence in client conversations — I can now discuss AI architecture, data sovereignty, and agent capabilities without hesitation or vagueness.
  • A structured positioning framework — I know which message to lead with for each persona and which technical detail to deploy for each objection.
  • Real examples that land — not "SAP has AI agents" but "the Cash Management Agent saves your finance team 70% of manual reconciliation time — here is exactly how."
  • A sustainability angle I was not expecting — the Green Ledger and Sustainability Tower capabilities genuinely surprised me with their depth and maturity.
  • A new appreciation for data — the courses reinforced that AI is a data story first and a technology story second. That reframing has changed how I approach every client engagement.
  • A structured positioning framework — I know which message to lead with for each persona and which technical detail to deploy for each objection.
  • Real examples that land — not "SAP has AI agents" but "the Cash Management Agent saves your finance team 70% of manual reconciliation time — here is exactly how."
  • A sustainability angle I was not expecting — the Green Ledger and Sustainability Tower capabilities genuinely surprised me with their depth and maturity.
  • A new appreciation for data — the courses reinforced that AI is a data story first and a technology story second. That reframing has changed how I approach every client engagement.

The most valuable moment in the entire certification was a scenario-based roleplay where I had to respond to a sceptical client who said "our ERP works fine, why change it?" Every concept from all three courses came together in that one moment. That is when I knew the learning had actually landed.

Final Thought

If you are considering this certification — do it. Not just for the badge, but for the way it reshapes how you think about AI, data, and enterprise transformation.

And when you sit down with your next client who says "we are curious about AI but not sure where to start" — you will know exactly what to say.

Start with the data. Layer the intelligence. Let the Flywheel turn.

If this was useful, please like and share. Questions or your own experiences? Drop them in the comments below. Happy to connect with fellow SAP learners.

entry.title: Re: Journal Entry Multiple Object Assignment with Statist...
entry.content:
Good day @S0025758586 

Thank you for the details.

Based on your description, that requirement will face real standard design limitation in Public Cloud. The reasons are:

  • A journal entry can carry the required account assignment object for the posting, e.g., cost center or WBS element.
  • For analysis, SAP distinguishes between real and statistical posting.

However, your proposed design needs two WBS dimension in the same posting, one real and one statistical and Public Cloud does support the standard pattern you described. SAP’s analytics and CO logic clearly distinguishes real versus statistical posting but that does not mean Public Cloud supports the same object twice in the account assignment block for your journal entry use case.

See the following reference for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/59d0135699bf8243e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/b050d7531a4d424de10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

 

So, for your example: “Actual to WBS X” and “Statistical to WBS Y”, this is not the target standard design for a simple manual journal entry in PC.

For the business need you described, the cleanest way is:

  • If your primary requirement is true project costing, then post the cost really to the revenue project WBS X and carry the extra tracking dimension such as plat number/Vehicle/lease object in a custom field for reporting. Then expose it in reporting using a custom CDS view or analytical query.

See the following reference for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/21f32a09ebf34882abbda7fecb758241.html?locale=en-US

  • Alternatively, if your primary goal is to track overhead, then post the cost really to the cost center and use a statistical WBS for project visibility. This is the standard pattern when the source of cost is overhead oriented rather than true direct project cost.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/73813f5862ee4442e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/59d0135699bf8243e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

  • I would not recommend trying to simulate the requirement by forcing a second WBS object into the same journal entry through a workaround. That would not align with the Public Cloud standard model and would be brittle.

 

Regarding your design question:

Question: Should we create a custom field to add another attribute or dimension to the journal entry and a CDS view for reporting?

Answer: Based on your wording, the requirement is useful but not mandatory. In that case, I suggest you use the real account assignment for the true accounting object and use a custom field for the extra tracking dimension.

So, in your vehicle lease example, the recommended design is:

  • Journal Entry 123; real posting to WBS X and cost element A for 100 EUR and additional custom field such as Vehicle Plate Number or Lease Tracking ID
  • Reporting built on journal entry data model, filtered by WBS X and analysed by that custom field.

That will ensure correct accounting, clean core compliance, the extra dimension you need for reporting and no attempt to duplicate WBS object usage in one posting.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/21f32a09ebf34882abbda7fecb758241.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/ca03bbfc892d4474be69934d6f5c783d.html?locale=en-US

Question:  should we seperate the statistical use of objects by the source of actual direct costs?

Answer: Yes, that would align with the standard guidelines. If your business event is naturally:

  • Cost center driven overhead, then use real cost center plus statistical WBS
  • Project direct cost, use real WBS and for the additional tracking need, use a custom field and not a second WBS.

That split is cleaner than you trying to force one universal posting model for all cases. It also aligns better with how standard reporting and CO objects are intended to be used in Public Cloud.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/73813f5862ee4442e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

 

To summarise:

  • The standard design does not support using two WBS elements in one journal entry line the way you described it.
  • For direct project cost, post the cost to the real WBS and use a custom field for extra tracking like vehicle plate number.
  • For overhead costs, post to the cost center and use a statistical WBS.
  • It is not advisable to force a workaround to imitate a second WBS in the same posting.
  • The simplest recommendation is: real accounting object for posting, custom field for extra reporting detail.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAP System Changes Required – Transition from Income-tax Act, 1961 to Income-tax Act, 2025 - Your ac
entry.content:
This is to bring in your kind notice that, the Income-tax Act, 2025 (along with Income-tax Rules, 2026) will replace the existing Income-tax Act, 1961 with effect from 1 April 2026. The new legislation introduces structural changes, including renumbering of sections, revised compliance framework based on the “Tax Year” concept, and rationalization of statutory Forms.

 

Currently section  length in sap showing 4 digits(194I). We required increase length of the section length in sap as per new Indian tax act. Please Let uu know if there any new sap notes or any other process available to increase the lenth.


--- Steps to Reproduce ---

This is to bring in your kind notice that, the Income-tax Act, 2025 (along with Income-tax Rules, 2026) will replace the existing Income-tax Act, 1961 with effect from 1 April 2026. The new legislation introduces structural changes, including renumbering of sections, revised compliance framework based on the “Tax Year” concept, and rationalization of statutory Forms.

 

Currently section  length in sap showing 4 digits(194I). We required increase length of the section length in sap as per new Indian tax act. Please Let uu know if there any new sap notes or any other process available to increase the lenth.

entry.title: Re: How to preview Excel file in SAPUI5 like PDFViewer?
entry.content:
Hi! This is a very common requirement. Since sap.m.PDFViewer relies on the browser's native ability to render PDFs, and browsers cannot natively render .xlsx files, there is no out-of-the-box SAPUI5 control for this.

However, you can achieve a perfect, interactive Excel preview (without forcing a download) by combining two powerful open-source libraries:

  1. SheetJS (XLSX): To parse the Base64 Excel string (or uploaded file) into JavaScript arrays.

  2. Handsontable: To render that array data into a beautiful, true-to-life spreadsheet UI right inside your Fiori app.

Here is the step-by-step implementation:

Before your UI5 app bootstraps, you need to load the SheetJS and Handsontable libraries via CDN. Add these lines inside the in your index.html file:

HTML
 
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/handsontable/dist/handsontable.full.min.js"></script>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/handsontable/dist/handsontable.full.min.css">

<script src="https://cdn.sheetjs.com/xlsx-latest/package/dist/xlsx.full.min.js"></script>

Handsontable needs a plain HTML to render itself. We can inject this into UI5 using the sap.ui.core.HTML control.

XML
 
<mvc:View controllerName="your.namespace.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:core="sap.ui.core">
    
    <Page title="Excel Previewer">
        <core:HTML id="excelContainer" 
            content='<div id="hot-container" style="width: 100%; height: 500px; overflow: hidden;"></div>'>
        </core:HTML>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>with filters and other functionality as same as excel

Here is the logic to take your data, parse it with SheetJS, and feed it into Handsontable.

(Note: The example below shows how to handle a File Upload, but if you have a Base64 string from OData, you simply pass the Base64 string directly into XLSX.read(base64String, {type: 'base64'}) instead of using a FileReader).

JavaScript
 
/* global XLSX, Handsontable */
sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
], function (Controller) {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("your.namespace.controller.View1", {

        onUpload: function (oEvent) {
            var oFile = oEvent.getParameter("files") ? oEvent.getParameter("files")[0] : oEvent;
            var reader = new FileReader();
            var that = this;
            var container = document.getElementById('hot-container');

            reader.onload = function (e) {
                // 1. Read the file data
                var data = new Uint8Array(e.target.result);
                var workbook = XLSX.read(data, { type: 'array' });
                
                // 2. Get the first sheet
                var sSheetName = workbook.SheetNames[0];
                
                // 3. Convert sheet to Array of Arrays (Required for Handsontable)
                var aJsonData = XLSX.utils.sheet_to_json(workbook.Sheets[sSheetName], {
                    header: 1,  // Forces an array format instead of objects
                    defval: ""  // Prevents empty cells from collapsing
                });

                that.filedata = aJsonData;
                console.log("Parsed Excel Data:", that.filedata);

                // 4. Initialize or update Handsontable
                if (that._oHot) {
                    // If table already exists, just load the new data
                    that._oHot.loadData(that.filedata);
                } else {
                    // Create the table for the first time
                    that._oHot = new Handsontable(container, {
                        data: that.filedata,
                        rowHeaders: true,
                        colHeaders: true,
                        height: 'auto',
                        autoWrapRow: true,
                        autoWrapCol: true,
                        licenseKey: 'non-commercial-and-evaluation', 
                        contextMenu: true,
                        filters: true,
                        dropdownMenu: true,
                        manualColumnResize: true,
                        manualRowResize: true,
                        copyPaste: true
                    });
                }
            };

            // Start reading the file
            if (oFile) {
                reader.readAsArrayBuffer(oFile);
            }
        }
    });
});

entry.title: Re: Revise the Business Partner Number Range Configuration
entry.content:
Good day @Chanaporn 

Thank you for your question.

If the number range interval was manually adjusted in the Production system, trying to transport it later from the Customizing or Quality system does not automatically overwrite that Production interval.

So, for example:

  • Customizing: Z1 = 0001000001 to 0001999999
  • Production: Z1 = 0010000001 to 0019999999

See the reference below for possible scenarios.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/012ed88da3624780b0c68f923404ddab.html?locale=en-US

If Production was manually changed, the later transported configuration does not simply reset Production back to the Customizing values.

In practice, what this typically means is that the important implication is that your system can become intentionally different for the same number range object. SAP allow number range specification such as intervals to be changed but the documented transport scenario for Public Cloud makes clear that the product system keeps its own productive number range instead of being blindly overwritten by the incoming transported setting.

See the following reference for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/4460d7531a4d424de10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

Question: If a transport request (TR) is later transported from the Customizing system to Test and then to Production, will the transported configuration overwrite the existing number range settings that were manually adjusted in the Production system?

Answer: No, the transported configuration will not overwrite the manually adjusted Production number range interval in the way you described.

 

The reason for that is, number ranges in Production are business critical and often need to remain stable in the productive tenant.

Be aware that:

  • Quality and Production may end up with different intervals
  • This is expected if you manually maintained Production
  • You should verify the productive interval after you have concluded importing, and clearly document the intended difference.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAP BTP AI best practices – Document grounding
entry.content:
AI conversations are only as good as the information behind them. And honestly, that’s where many projects get stuck.

In the next session of our SAP BTP AI best practices series, we’ll explore something incredibly practical: document grounding on SAP BTP.

Think of it as giving AI access to the knowledge your enterprise already owns; policies, manuals, product documentation, internal guides, and making that information usable through out-of-the-box RAG workflows.

During this webinar, we’ll walk through the architecture of SAP AI Core, highlight the capabilities that make document grounding work, and share a few real-world examples of how partners are already putting it into action.

The goal?  Help you make enterprise data accessible in a way that feels simple, scalable, and honestly… very powerful.

If you’re building AI solutions on SAP BTP, this is one session you’ll want on your calendar.

Register now and join us.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013266.html
